_readme: Site changelog, rendered by changelog.html. Newest entry FIRST. Every meaningful commit to v1-discovery-map gets an entry (the pre-commit hook enforces this). Write 'summary' in plain language for the Arts Council — what changed for a visitor, not implementation detail. 'commit' is the short hash; the page links it to GitHub.

entries.title: Every website link checked: 13 repaired, 65 dead links retired
entries.summary: All 831 venue website links were tested. 646 work fine. 13 had small address problems (a missing www or an outdated link) that we repaired automatically. 65 led to vanished pages or domains that no longer exist; place cards no longer offer those links, so visitors stop landing on error pages. 55 borderline cases (sites that block automated checks or have certificate problems) are held in a review list rather than being removed.

entries.title: 103 more places switched to photos from their own websites
entries.summary: Hundreds of place photos were still borrowed from Google links that expire over time. We checked all 581 places that have both a Google-linked photo and their own website, and for 103 of them the venue's own site offered a usable photo. Those are now stored with the map permanently and credited to the venue. That brings self-hosted, never-expiring photos to 117 places, and every one is the image the venue chose to represent itself.

entries.title: Two contentless map pins retired pending real information
entries.summary: A sweep for places with nothing to show a visitor (template description, no photo, no website, no events) found only four. Two unverified ones no longer get a map pin (they remain findable in the directory and are easy to restore once real content arrives). The other two are public-art entries held for the Arts Council to decide. Nearly everything on the map has at least one piece of real content, which is better than expected.

entries.title: 14 places now show photos from the venues' own websites
entries.summary: For places that lost their photo, we checked the venue's own website for the picture it chooses to represent itself (the image a site declares for social sharing). 159 venue sites were checked; 14 yielded a usable photo, now stored with the map itself so it can never expire, each credited to the venue's website. Logos and graphics were filtered out automatically. Most small venue sites simply don't publish a share photo, which is why the long-term plan is a community photo drive.

entries.title: Broken photos replaced with honest placeholders
entries.summary: Many place photos were hot-linked from Google and those links expire. We checked all 1,065 of them: 856 still work, 209 had quietly died and were showing as broken images. Those 209 places now show a clean category placeholder with a 'photo not yet sourced' note instead of a broken picture. Next step: pulling real photos from the venues' own websites.

entries.title: Map accuracy audit: 6 misplaced pins corrected, 48 relabeled as approximate
entries.summary: Every public place with a street address (946 of them) was cross-checked against the US Census geocoder. 764 matched within 150 meters. Six pins that were 2-25 km from their real address (including Art Hike and the Gold Country Celtic Society) now sit at the correct location. Forty-eight pins we could not verify keep their spot but are honestly marked as approximate until reviewed. The check is a repeatable script, so we can re-audit any time the data changes.

entries.title: Two small delights: Surprise me nearby, and Before-or-after stops on events
entries.summary: A 'Surprise me nearby' button in Places mode pulls up a handful of under-the-radar stops described in their own words, with a re-roll. Event cards now list the closest eat/drink/shop stops so an event can become an evening. Both are plain distance and real venue text — no invented curation.

entries.title: Events mode gets a time lens: Today, This weekend, Next 7 days
entries.summary: A chip row in Events mode narrows both the list and the map markers to the window you care about. Events-only by design: the map has no opening-hours data yet, so there is deliberately no Open Now filter.

entries.title: Place cards now connect everything: events, MUSE, paths, and what is nearby
entries.summary: Selecting a place now shows its upcoming events (tap to open), the MUSE Magazine stories it appears in, the curated paths it belongs to (tap to walk the route), and the closest cultural stops around it. Venue descriptions sourced from the venue itself now say so, with a link.

entries.title: The map now greets you like a host, not a database
entries.summary: The panel opens with a featured cultural anchor — photo, its story hook, and a one-tap way in — followed by a 'What are you in the mood for?' browse list. Search is still there, just no longer the front door.

entries.title: Map markers redesigned: named anchors first, calmer dots, live-event halos
entries.summary: At county zoom the map now leads with named cultural anchors instead of ~700 equal dots; ordinary places fade in as you zoom into town. Dot colors moved to a muted ink-leaning palette so red is reserved for what is live or selected, and any place with an upcoming event now carries a soft red halo in every mode.

entries.title: 219 places now describe themselves in their own words
entries.summary: We visited the websites of 452 places that still had placeholder text and copied how each business describes itself — verbatim, from their own homepage or About page. 219 cards now carry the venue's own voice (the Holbrooke's "We've been building character since 1852," for example). Every quote was machine-verified against the source page; nothing was reworded or invented. A side benefit: we found 88 broken venue links and one hijacked website, now logged for follow-up.

entries.title: This page — a public update log
entries.summary: The site now has this “What's New” page (linked in the top menu) so the Arts Council and community can see every update at a glance. Each entry links to the exact code change for anyone who wants the technical detail.

entries.title: Usability fix round 1 — 11 issues from a full site audit
entries.summary: We tested the whole site like a first-time visitor (on a computer and a phone) and fixed everything we found. Phone users get a proper menu button now, and a bug that could trap phone users on the map is gone.
details: New: menu button on phones — MUSE Magazine, Culture Forward, Current Map, and Proposed Flows are reachable on mobile
New: the Events tab now shows a browsable, date-sorted list of all upcoming events
Improved: place search moved to the top of the panel, with results right under the box
Improved: routes now have a back button, and route copy says four routes (there are four)
Fixed: on phones, the detail card could cover the whole screen with no way to close it
Fixed: detail cards opened mid-scroll instead of at the top
Fixed: map labels no longer draw on top of the detail card; map credits no longer hidden

entries.title: Site goes live + Proposed Flows gallery
entries.summary: The map got its own public web address (v1-discovery-map.vercel.app) and a new Proposed Flows page showing design directions for where the project could go next.

entries.title: Live community events, refreshed daily
entries.summary: Events on the map are now real and current — pulled automatically every day from nine community calendars (Arts Council, library, county, KVMR, GVDA, and local venues including Crazy Horse, Golden Era, and Bodhi Hive).

entries.title: Friendlier wording across the site
entries.summary: All on-screen text was rewritten in a warmer, citizen voice — less database jargon, more like a local pointing you somewhere good.

entries.title: Cleaner place data
entries.summary: Duplicate place entries were merged and the map's data tooling was reworked, so each venue appears once with its best information.

entries.title: Real street map + places snapped to rooftops
entries.summary: The background map switched to a clear street map, and 411 places whose locations were only estimates were moved onto their actual buildings.

entries.title: Browse by Outing Type
entries.summary: A new way in: pick a lane like Art, History, or Local Shops and the map narrows to match, with counts shown for each.

entries.title: One trustworthy place dataset
entries.summary: All place lists were combined into a single canonical dataset with honest confidence tiers — solid markers for verified locations, hollow for estimates.
